artifacts.content:
Hi Jenna,

Kids are finally in bed and I’m icing my back on the couch, so it seems like a good time to actually answer your last message instead of just heart‑reacting it.

I saw Mark Delaney at Bartlett Regional’s outpatient rehab clinic again this morning (every other Thursday like clockwork). He was pretty blunt this time – said I’m “one bad lift away from a herniated disc” if I keep hauling coolers and gear myself on those peak cruise days. So, yeah, your subject line from March – “Might have to cut back a tour slot 😕” – is starting to feel less hypothetical and more like doctor’s orders.

The timing just feels brutal with this proposed extra $7 per passenger head tax hanging over us for 2026. I already ran the numbers on a 10‑person whale watch – that’s $70 a departure before we even talk fuel, dock, insurance, and the usual passenger fees. I can’t just “do more myself” to make up the difference when Mark’s telling me to stop pretending I’m still 28 and bulletproof.

On top of that, Mom’s heart stuff is still in the back of my mind. I keep flashing back to February, flying down to Bellingham and sitting with her and Dr. Anita Chow at PeaceHealth St. Joseph’s for that follow‑up cardiology appointment after her little cardiac scare. She keeps saying she’s fine, but I’m the one lying awake at 3 a.m. wondering, “What happens if Mom has another episode right in the middle of July cruise madness?” There’s no easy way to disappear from Juneau for a week in peak season.

Anyway, enough doom. Sophie’s cross‑country meet is Saturday at Savikko, and Caleb is insisting we make orca cupcakes afterward (no idea how to pull that off). If you’re free Friday, kids are with Eric and I could definitely use a beer at Devil’s Club or a lazy movie night.

Thanks for always letting me vent about boats and backs and parents.

Love,
Laura

metadata.subject: Back, boats, and everything in between

artifacts.content:
Hi Alan,

Catching up on a quiet, rainy Sunday and wanted to circle back on a few money things before we get swallowed by early bookings.

First, can you send me an updated amortization on the SBA Economic Injury Disaster Loan I took through Northrim back in 2020? Last time we talked (your October 2023 email, “Re: EIDL balance + vessel upgrade timing”), we were sitting at about a $38,000 balance after I deferred that one quarterly payment to cover the emergency engine repairs on Aurora Spirit. I just want to see where we’ll be by end of 2025 if I keep paying at the current rate versus accelerating a bit.

Second, I’m still stewing on the proposed seasonal passenger head tax increase. If that extra $7 per cruise passenger on independent excursions actually lands the way CBJ is talking, my quick math says we’re looking at a 6–8 point hit to margins on our standard $179 whale/glacier tour unless we bump prices to the $186–$189 range. That’s before we factor in ShoreTripPro’s commission and the credit card fees.

Speaking of ShoreTripPro, I’m still a little rattled from that informal inquiry from the CBJ Sales Tax Office last August about our 2022 seasonal passenger fee remittances. The whole “who is the seller of record” question on those platform bookings is still fuzzy to me. In my email to you then (“CBJ audit question – ShoreTripPro commissions”), I think I estimated we could be on the hook for $4–5K in back‑owed fees and penalties if they decided we calculated wrong. Have you heard anything more definitive from them, or should we assume that risk is still hanging out there?

Given all of the above, I’m trying to decide whether it’s insane to keep the 2026 vessel upgrade for Taku Spirit on the table. Between the EIDL balance, potential CBJ penalties, and the new head tax, I don’t want to get upside‑down.

Happy to swing by your office later this month if a face‑to‑face is easier.

Thanks,
Laura

metadata.subject: EIDL balance, CBJ inquiry, and head tax math

artifacts.content:
Title: 2025–2026 worst‑case planning – do not share

1. Personal draw / household
- If the head tax passes in current form for 2026, assume 10–15% drop in volume + margin compression.
- Worst‑case spreadsheet from October 2024 (“2025 worst‑case staffing cuts”) shows me dropping my own draw to $2,000/month for the 2026 season.
- Would need to lean on personal line of credit at Alaska USA Federal Credit Union to cover:
  • Rent on 411 Harbor View Drive, Apt 4B
  • Sophie’s orthodontist payments at Juneau Family Orthodontics (ask them about stretching payment plan if needed).
- Emotional note: this is not sustainable long‑term, but maybe survivable for one brutal season if it keeps staff employed.

2. Staffing / ownership
- Reduce seasonal staff from 18 to ~12 guides/deckhands.
- Cut paid training hours but keep minimum safety/interpretive standards.
- Revisit March 2024 coalition notes (“Coalition – contingency ideas”):
  • Serious conversation with Michael Han (Anchorage) about selling up to a 40% stake in Northern Lights Adventure Tours.
  • Upside: capital for new fuel‑efficient vessel.
  • Downside: pressure to sign exclusive contract with a major cruise line I’ve been publicly criticizing. Could change the culture of the company.

3. Insurance / risk
- Remember July 2022 near‑miss at AJ Dock (junior captain bumped piling with Taku Spirit).
- Pacific Marine Underwriters warning letter required:
  • Extra paid training
  • Temporary passenger capacity reduction on Taku Spirit
- Internal memo to staff at the time: “Insurance compliance – PLEASE READ” – we truly cannot afford another claim before 2025 renewal.
- New taxes + any future incident = no cushion.

4. Action items
- Update worst‑case spreadsheet quarterly.
- Quietly sound out Michael re: terms that don’t force cruise‑line exclusivity.
- Build in extra training days pre‑season to avoid any repeat of 2022.
- Keep this note for my eyes only.

Hi Ingrid,

Thanks again for talking me off the ledge on Sunday. I figured I’d write everything down while it’s still fresh in my head.

I keep thinking back to that November 2021 mediated session at the Juneau Community Justice Center, when we had to drag in the calendar and renegotiate the parenting plan because Eric missed those two Thursday pickups after overtime at Alaska Seaplane Maintenance. Then that Harborview pickup where he showed up smelling like alcohol… I still remember typing that December 2021 email to Megan Ross at Ross & Caldwell (“Re: revised custody calendar”) with my hands shaking. I don’t want to go back to that level of conflict.

The structure we landed on mostly works now – kids with me Monday–Thursday nights during the school year, alternating weekends – but it also means I can’t just add late evening tours to chase revenue if this new head tax goes through. There’s not much flexibility baked in.

Layer on top of that Mom’s health. Flying down to Bellingham in February to sit with her and Dr. Anita Chow at PeaceHealth St. Joseph for that follow‑up cardiology appointment really rattled me more than I let on. I’ve been losing sleep wondering what happens if she has another episode in the middle of July when we’re running three departures a day.

And then there’s Sophie. Since January she’s been seeing Rachel Kim at Juneau Youth Services twice a month for anxiety – mostly about the downtown crowds and, honestly, about “Mom losing the boat business if the city keeps raising fees.” I even emailed her school counselor, Dana Carlson, in May (“Sophie missing practice + tourism meetings”) to explain why she was skipping cross‑country some afternoons when I had to be at tourism meetings.

I feel like the head‑tax debate has seeped into every corner of our lives: custody logistics, my mom’s cardiology follow‑ups, Sophie’s counseling sessions. Meanwhile, Caleb just wants more time with orcas.

Anyway, thank you for listening and for not judging Eric when I vent. The kids adore their Aunt Ingrid, and so do I.

Love,
Laura

Event: Mom cardiology follow‑up – Bellingham

Date: 2024-02-22
Time: 10:30 a.m. – 1:30 p.m. (Pacific)
Location: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, Cardiology Clinic – with Dr. Anita Chow
Attendees: Me, Mom (Karen Miller)

Notes:
- Fly SEA > BLI night before, stay at Mom and Dad’s.
- Bring list of Mom’s meds + notes from her mild cardiac scare last fall.
- Questions for Dr. Chow:
  • How likely is another episode in next 1–2 years?
  • Any restrictions that would make it hard for Mom to fly to Juneau in summer?
  • Warning signs Dad should watch for.

Tourism/work tie‑ins:
- If prognosis is uncertain, I need to plan for possibility of another emergency trip mid‑season (July/August) when cruise traffic is insane.
- Factor into 2025–2026 staffing plans – can’t be the only one who can cover every departure.

Personal:
- I’ve been losing sleep over this, especially with the head‑tax mess brewing and how thinly stretched I already feel.
- Block off time after appointment to walk the waterfront with Mom if she’s up for it.
- Call Sophie and Caleb after we’re done; reassure them Nana is okay.

Reminder: check in with Ingrid later about how much she can help if I need to duck out of Juneau for a few days mid‑season.

metadata.title: Mom cardiology follow‑up – Bellingham
metadata.location: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, Bellingham, WA

artifacts.content:
Thread: “Summer schedule + Mom’s heart stuff”

Ingrid: You got the draft cruise calendar yet? Trying to figure out when to visit you vs. when to hide in Anchorage 😂

Me: Lol. Same. Just got the tentative schedule and it’s wall‑to‑wall ships mid‑July.

Me: Honestly I’m more stressed about Mom than the ships right now.

Ingrid: How did the PeaceHealth appointment go? You never really said.

Me: February trip was rough. Sitting in that exam room at St. Joseph’s with Dr. Chow talking about “mild cardiac events” while I’m mentally counting how many July departures I can cancel if something happens again.

Me: I keep thinking, what if Mom has another episode right in the middle of July cruise madness? I can’t just walk away from the harbor for a week.

Ingrid: You’d walk away. We’d figure it out.

Me: Maybe. But between the proposed head tax, the EIDL I’m still paying down, and that CBJ Sales Tax Office inquiry last August about the ShoreTripPro remittances (remember, I told you it could be like $4–5K if they decide I calculated wrong), the idea of canceling tours makes my stomach hurt.

Ingrid: Ugh. That’s a lot.

Me: And then there’s Sophie. She’s still seeing Rachel Kim at Juneau Youth Services twice a month. Her big fear is literally “Mom losing the boat business if the city keeps raising fees.”

Me: I hate that she even knows what a passenger head tax is.

Ingrid: She also knows her mom is stubborn as hell and takes care of people.

Me: Trying. Right now I just feel like I’m juggling Nana’s heart, city politics, and orca facts for Caleb.

Ingrid: One day at a time, cousin.

Me: Yeah. One ship at a time too.

Hey [Jenna],

I need to say this out loud to someone who isn’t in the tourism bubble.

You know how I’ve been going to those community tourism meetings? On a few Fridays earlier this year, when the kids were with Eric, I started grabbing a beer at Devil’s Club Brewing with David McCormick after the sessions. He’s on the Assembly, and at first it was just debriefing policy stuff – head tax scenarios, harbor infrastructure, all that.

But by late March the conversations had definitely drifted past policy. In that March 29 text I sent you (“This is getting messy”), I wasn’t exaggerating. There was a brief romantic thing, nothing dramatic, but in a town the size of Juneau, it feels like the whole world.

Now every time the head‑tax ordinance comes up at an Assembly meeting, I’m hyper‑aware that people might think I’m getting some kind of preferential treatment because I had a few too many IPAs with him this spring. The irony is he’s actually been more cautious about talking to me since then, which is probably the right call, but it leaves me without someone on the inside I can be totally candid with.

Combine that with the financial stress (still carrying that $38K‑ish SBA EIDL balance, worst‑case spreadsheet saying I might have to cut my own draw to $2,000/month in 2026 and lean on the Alaska USA line of credit just to cover Harbor View rent and Sophie’s braces), and I feel like I’m walking around with a neon sign over my head that says “Conflict of interest + broke.”

I’m trying to keep all of this separate from the public fight over the tax, but it’s getting harder. I don’t want the coalition to feel blindsided if rumors start.

Anyway, thanks for being my non‑tourism, non‑politics friend who still listens to all the tourism and politics.

Love,
L

Hi Mom,

I hope you're doing well in Bellingham and taking it easy after your recent health scare. We've been thinking about you a lot, and I try to remind myself to enjoy the little moments with Sophie and Caleb, especially now that summer is winding down.

Things have been busy here in Juneau. I'm still juggling everything with Northern Lights Adventure Tours, and we're facing some challenges. There's a proposed increase in the seasonal passenger head tax, which is a statewide issue, and it's causing us small operators quite a bit of concern. I'm committed to supporting efforts that protect our community, but it's tough when these costs could potentially impact my business so deeply.

I'm grateful for the supportive network here, though. Our coalition of small tour companies is actively engaged, and we're trying to make our voices heard. It's important that we stick together to ensure that sustainable tourism remains viable in Southeast Alaska. Thankfully, I am a current ATIA member, which gives me a platform to advocate for our interests more effectively.

On the home front, Sophie is thriving at Floyd Dryden Middle School and just joined the cross-country team. She's even talking about wanting her own camera to take wildlife photos on our tours. Caleb, meanwhile, is as fascinated as ever with orcas and loves sharing fun facts about them.

Eric and I have kept our co-parenting routine steady, with the kids staying with me Monday through Thursday. We're making it work, and I'm glad to have some structure amidst the chaos. Our apartment at 411 Harbor View Drive, Suite 203, has a beautiful view that never fails to bring a sense of peace, even on the busiest days.

I try to find moments for myself, too, attending yoga classes and focusing on long-term planning for the business. I'm looking into more fuel-efficient vessel options and researching grants for sustainable tourism. It's a balancing act, but I'm hopeful we can navigate these changes positively.

Please give my best to Dad. I look forward to our next video call and catching up more. Give yourselves a big hug from us, and let me know if there's anything you need.

Love,

Laura

Tel: (907) 555-2834
Email: laura.jensen@nlatours.com

metadata.subject: Updates from Juneau and the Kids
